title: cliff_high_angle_rescue
description: A hiker has fallen and is stranded on a narrow ledge 25 metres below the cliff top with a suspected spinal injury.

timeline.title: Hiker Stranded on Ledge
timeline.description: Hiker fell during descent and landed on narrow ledge 25 m below cliff edge. Conscious, complaining of neck pain.

timeline.title: Anchor System Established
timeline.description: Two independent rope systems established at cliff top. Litter team preparing for lower.

timeline.title: Rescuer Lowered to Ledge
timeline.description: Medical rescuer lowered on main line. Spinal precautions applied. Victim packaged in Stokes litter.

timeline.title: Litter Raised
timeline.description: Litter and attendant raised to cliff top using haul system. Road-head medical team receives victim.

timeline.title: Victim Transported
timeline.description: Victim transported to hospital by EMS with spinal precautions maintained. Scene demobilised.

node-1.title: Hiker on Cliff Ledge
node-1.situation: A hiker has fallen during a cliff descent and landed on a ledge 25 metres below the top edge. They are conscious, reporting severe neck pain, and unable to move. Bystanders on the cliff top are considering lowering a climbing rope to the victim.
node-1.question: How do you establish a safe rescue operation?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop improvised lowering, establish a formal incident command, brief the cliff edge for exclusion zone compliance, and begin setting up a two-rope (main and belay) rescue system with independent anchors.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow bystanders to lower a climbing rope — the victim needs to be reached immediately.

options.label: C
options.text: Ask the victim to try to climb up to the cliff top using a rope.

node-2.title: Anchor System Assessment
node-2.situation: The anchor area at the cliff top has soft soil and only one suitable rock anchor within 3 metres of the edge. A large boulder 6 metres back is available. The team has a load-sharing anchor kit.
node-2.question: How do you set up the anchor system?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the boulder 6 metres back as the primary anchor with a load-sharing extension to the edge; set up a separate independent belay anchor — no single-point anchors over the edge.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the single rock anchor near the edge — it is faster and looks solid.

options.label: C
options.text: Anchor to the bumper of a parked truck — it is close and heavy.

node-3.title: Rescuer Lowered to Victim
node-3.situation: A medical rescuer has been lowered to the ledge. The victim is conscious with a suspected C-spine injury and a possible femur fracture. The ledge is 60 cm wide. The rescuer must perform a medical assessment and package the victim for rescue.
node-3.question: How does the rescuer manage spinal precautions on the ledge?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply manual in-line stabilisation immediately, fit a cervical collar, log-roll the victim onto a Stokes litter with full spinal precautions, and secure all straps before initiating the haul.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow the victim to sit upright in a harness for the rescue — it is faster than packaging them in a litter.

options.label: C
options.text: Skip the cervical collar and package the victim in a quick-wrap body harness to save time.

node-4.title: Haul System Deployment
node-4.situation: The victim is packaged in the litter with the rescuer attached as a litter attendant. The haul team at the top must raise approximately 120 kg total. A 3:1 mechanical advantage system is set up.
node-4.question: What is the correct haul protocol?

options.label: A
options.text: Maintain constant communication between the litter attendant and haul team, raise steadily with the belay rope tracking simultaneously, and keep the litter horizontal throughout the raise.

options.label: B
options.text: Haul as fast as possible to minimise time on the cliff face.

options.label: C
options.text: Allow the litter to rotate freely — the attendant can adjust position as needed.

node-5.title: Victim at Cliff Top
node-5.situation: The victim has been raised to the cliff top. EMS is ready. The victim is conscious, with worsening neck pain, tingling in both hands, and reduced grip strength — increasing concern for spinal cord injury.
node-5.question: How do you transfer the victim to EMS?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep the victim in the litter with all spinal precautions maintained; transfer directly onto the EMS stretcher using a log-roll technique with C-spine held manually.

options.label: B
options.text: Remove the victim from the litter and assist them to walk to the ambulance — they are conscious.

node-6.title: Scenario Complete
node-6.situation: The victim was successfully rescued with full spinal precautions maintained throughout. A proper two-rope anchor system prevented rope failure. EMS transported the victim with appropriate C-spine immobilisation, and the neurosurgery team was pre-alerted. No rescuer injuries occurred.
